observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant to initially record all acquisitions of supplies, materials and goods funded from the LDRRMF in the appropriate inventory accounts; and
b. General Services Officer to prepare the RSMI duly supported with RIS for the issued supplies and submit them to the Accounting Office for proper recording to expenses accounts.
observations.managementAction: No Management action was taken.
observations.reason: The Management did not comment on the reason of non-implementation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) instruct the CGSO to immediately submit the RPCPPE for the physical count of
PPEs conducted in CY 2022, and in coordination with the City Accountant,
gradually reconcile any discrepancy with the General Ledger;
b) direct the City Accountant to exert extra effort to locate records pertaining to
PPEs acquired prior to CY 2006, which are also necessary for the conduct of one-
time cleaning of PPEs; and
c) enjoin the members of the Inventory Committee to speed up the preparation and
actual conduct of physical inventory activities for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE
to lessen, if not totally eliminate, the discrepancy between actually existing and
recorded PPEs.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to compute and
journalize the depreciation expenses of the PPE accounts totaling P986,068,575.49, to
ensure that the relevant accounts are presented fairly in the financial statements of
the City, in compliance with Section 262 of GAM for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to compute and
make the necessary journal entries to adjust the Government Equity and
Accumulated Depreciation - Motor Vehicle accounts, and henceforth, ensure that all
Property, Plant and Equipment purchased by the City, and the corresponding
depreciation, if applicable, are recognized in the manner provided under Sections 4
and 120 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to adopt the
Perpetual Inventory Method where purchases of inventories not for immediate
distribution shall first be recorded under proper Inventory account, and to recognize
expense only upon issuances thereof to end-users and submission of necessary
supporting documents.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a) facilitate the completion of the documents needed to file the request for write-off
to the COA of the receivable due from the deceased former Municipal Treasurer
amounting to P1,088,560.00, which had been dormant and collectability of which
could no longer be ascertained, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated
December 19, 2016; and
b) exert effort to know the whereabouts of the former Cashier and determine the
possibility of collecting the amount due totaling P2,980,194.90, send demand
letters, exhaust legal remedies and, if efforts remained futile, likewise request
authority to write-off the dormant amount.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to cause the
immediate reversion of all long outstanding payables aged over two years to the
unappropriated surplus in the General Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor, being the Chairman of the Board of Trustees
(BoT) of ONI, require the:
a) Head of the Accounting/Finance Section of ONI to immediately reconstruct and
submit to the BoT the accounting reports and the financial statements of the ONI
during the last two or three years to enable evaluation of its financial performance
and condition over the years, and as basis for introduction of new or
modifications of existing policies and strategies to eventually capacitate the ONI
to be self-reliant or less dependent from the City for financing its operations;
b) Head of the Accounting/Finance Section of ONI to immediately start setting up
the books of accounts of the ONI, and thereafter, prepare and submit regularly to
the City Accounting Office and the Commission on Audit the hospital’s complete
quarterly and annual financial statements for calendar year (CY) 2023; and
c) Chief of Hospital of the ONI to prepare and submit to the Sanggunian Panlungsod
for approval, the Annual Operating Budget for ONI for CY 2024, using historical
data and estimates of the hospital’s own revenues and expenses as basis.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) direct the City Accountant to record the appropriate Receivable account to
recognize the overpayment made to the NFA amounting to P275,000.00 with
corresponding credit to Government Equity;
b) enjoin the CDRRMO and City Treasurer to write a letter to the NFA to explain the
circumstances behind the overpayment of P275,000.00 and request for the refund
of the amount; and
c) demand from the responsible City official to pay the P275,000.00 to the City
should recovery from the NFA be unsuccessful, otherwise, the amount will be
disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the:
a) CGSO to exert effort to locate the records of the 30 undocumented unserviceable
motor vehicles and/or conduct inquiry to establish the ownership of the said
vehicles and submit the results thereof to the Audit Team for verification and
evaluation;
b) concerned accountable officers to submit the duly accomplished forms required
for disposal to facilitate the determination by the Disposal Committee of their
condition; and
c) Members of Disposal Committee to immediately conduct the necessary Inspection
of the unserviceable Motor Vehicles and proceed with the disposal thereof.
Thereafter, prepare and submit the duly accomplished IIRUP supported with
complete disposal documents to the City Accountant for review and dropping of
the disposed properties from the accounting books.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to submit immediately
an explanation or justification on the excessive amounts of several work items in the
contract cost of the infrastructure project, for evaluation of the Audit Team,
otherwise, the amount of P26,578,692.38 will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) thru the City Legal Officer, demand from the supplier of the e-trikes, the
concerned GSO personnel and/or other former City officials who certified or
signed the AIP attesting to the completeness of the delivery of the 77 e-trikes to
 the City of Imus, Cavite on November 10, 2022, to submit immediately to the
COA Audit Team a written explanation why the amount of P40,656,000.00
should not be refunded or disallowed in audit in view of the findings of the COA
Audit Team of irregular disbursements of funds in connection with the e-trike
procurement;
b) thru the City Legal Officer, demand from the former GSO Head and former City
Mayor to submit to the COA Audit Team a written satisfactory explanation, for
further evaluation, why the project “Implementation of E-vehicles” was not
implemented as planned or in accordance with the City Ordinance No. 03-125,
Series of 2019 also known as the ELM Program which supported the City’s
application for loans to the Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP), otherwise,
the amount of P40,656,000.00 will be disallowed in audit;
c) instruct the City Accountant to compute the liquidated damages that the supplier
must pay due to late deliveries of the 77 e-trikes and demand immediate
payment from the supplier, and should the total sum of liquidated damages
exceed ten percent (10%) of the total contract price of P40,656,000.00, we
recommended that the City Mayor initiate the rescission of the contract, demand
the refund of the payments made to the supplier and impose appropriate sanctions
over and above the liquidated damages, to avoid disallowance in audit;
d) file appropriate civil and/or criminal charges and initiate blacklisting proceedings
against the supplier, if warranted, pursuant to the guidelines issued by the
Government Procurement and Policy Board; and
e) require the present City GSO to conduct investigation on the withdrawal of 24 e-
trikes on June 30, 2022 and the removal of batteries of the remaining 53 e-trikes
and impose appropriate sanctions against personnel who may be found liable for
releasing the batteries without proper authority and documentation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned Schools Division
Superintendent, BAC and the BAC-TWG to provide valid explanation or justification
in writing on the excessive ABC and unit price of the DSLR cameras, for evaluation
of the Audit Team, otherwise, the amount of P4,267,569.95 will be disallowed in
audit and filing of appropriate charges against accountable officials will be
recommended.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) concerned Schools Division Superintendent, BAC and the BAC-TWG to provide
valid explanation or justification in writing on the excessive ABC and unit price of
the noise cancelling headsets; and
b) GSO personnel concerned to submit a valid explanation or justification in writing
on why the subject items were accepted despite non-conformance with
specifications in the Purchase Order, for evaluation of the Audit Team, otherwise,
the amount of P891,662.30 will be disallowed in audit, and filing of appropriate
charges against accountable officials will be recommended.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) require the City Engineer and the City Accountant to compute the liquidated
damages equivalent to the delays of completion of the concerned Contractors,
and demand payment thereof to avoid audit disallowance; and
b) require the City Engineer and the City Accountant to strictly impose and deduct
liquidated damages equivalent to the delays of the Contractors before final
payment is made.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and other concerned
officials to prepare and submit the necessary requirements to enable the City to
include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of its JO
workers.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CDRRMO to ensure that
distribution of various supplies, materials and relief goods are supported by original
copy of the List of Recipients with their names, addresses, contact numbers, and
signatures, and the description and quantity of the items they had received to
facilitate validation by the COA Audit Team and other stakeholders, otherwise,
relevant disbursements may be suspended or disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the LDRRM Council to identify and
incorporate under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP the projects and activities to
be charged out of the unexpended LDRRMF, and ensure that the object of
expenditure of each PPA in LDRRMFIP is also presented therein.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Officer in Charge (OIC)-City
Planning and Development Council (CPDC) and other concerned officials to
thoroughly plan the start and target completion dates and timetable of the
procurement of all development projects, and integrate them in the AIP and APP to
facilitate timely completion of the projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the GFPS, through the GAD Focal
Person, to ensure that all PPAs included in the GPB have well-defined gender issues
or GAD mandate to be addressed.

conduct of physical count of PPE, and

The City Mayor shall:
• create an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less, and who shall be temporarily relieved of all their regular duties to devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until the same is completed, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

The Inventory Committee shall be required to perform the following:
• prepare the PIP to be submitted to the City Mayor for his approval to ensure that the conduct of inventory is properly and adequately planned;
• submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities; and
• conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the City in accordance with the approved PIP and prepare the corresponding RPCPPE complete with all the required information; and

b. Non-provision of We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to compute and journalize the depreciation expense to various Infrastructure Assets and to reclassify the erroneously classified infrastructure assets and buildings amounting to P216,776,852.18 to the appropriate PPE accounts and provide the corresponding accumulated depreciation expenses.
observations.managementAction:
The City Mayor created the Inventory Committee for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE account, through Executive Order No. 20 dated September 19, 2022. However, as of audit date, the Audit Team had not yet received an approved copy of PIP that will signify the official start of actual inventory taking for the said purpose.

The City Accountant has submitted the copy of Journal Entry Vouchers to reclassify to the appropriate PPE Account various Infrastructure Assets amounting to P216,776,852.18.
observations.reason: As of audit date, the Audit Team had not yet received an approved copy of PIP that will signify the official start of actual inventory taking for the said purpose.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to coordinate with the DBP for the updated amortization schedules, whenever there are drawdowns of loans, to assist her in the monitoring of the long-term loans payables of the City and to reclassify as current liabilities all amortizations due and payable within twelve months after the reporting date, in accordance with IPSAS 1, to ensure fair presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management has coordinated with the DBP for the updated amortization schedule. Likewise, the City Accountant has properly reclassified as current liabilities in the Financial Statement of the City.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor ensure that all procurements in the ensuing year should be meticulously and judiciously planned and shall:
a. direct all the end-user/implementing units of the procuring entity to prepared their respective PPMP within the prescribed timeline of the preparation of the annual budget to facilitate their timely review by the City Budget Office and consolidation into an indicative APP by the BAC Secretariat;
b. require the BAC Secretariat to prepare the indicative APP using the APP form prescribed by the GPPB, which will be submitted to the BAC for the final recommendation of the appropriate procurement modality, and finally to the HoPE for his approval of the APP; and
c. ensure that the priority development programs/projects to be funded from the 20% Development Fund in the succeeding year are adequately planned in terms of their procurement-and-implementation readiness to ensure optimal utilization of the fund.
observations.managementAction:
All end-user/implementing units prepared their PPMP. The BAC Secretariat prepared the indicative APP using the APP form prescribed by the GPPB. The BAC submitted the approved APP to the GPPB.

No Management action was taken.
observations.reason: No Management action was taken.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. instruct the City Accountant to compute the liquidated damages that the supplier must pay due to late deliveries of the 77 e-trikes and demand immediate payment from the supplier, and should the total sum of liquidated damages exceed ten percent (10%) of the total contract price of P40,656,000.00, we recommended that the City Mayor initiate the rescission of the contract, demand the refund of the payments made to the supplier and impose appropriate sanctions over and above the liquidated damages; and
b. file appropriate civil and/or criminal charges and initiate blacklisting proceedings against the supplier, if warranted, pursuant to the guidelines issued by the Government Procurement and Policy Board; and
c. require the present City GSO to conduct investigation on the withdrawal of 24 e-trikes on June 30, 2022 and the removal of batteries of the remaining 53 e-trikes and impose appropriate sanctions against personnel who may be found liable for releasing the batteries without proper authority and documentation.
observations.managementAction: The responsible persons submitted their justification to the Audit Team and was evaluated by the latter.
observations.reason: The amount of P40,656,000.00 was disallowed in audit per Notice of Disallowance (ND) No. 2023-001 (22) dated January 16, 2023 as the transaction was found to be attended by several irregularities as discussed in Part II.

observations.managementAction: The Officer in charge of the City IT Unit conducted a thorough assessment of the City’s existing database of households/families and the policies and procedures in the preparation of list of beneficiaries to determine the actual issues that could affect the integrity of the database and generated reports, and to recommend appropriate measures to ensure that the problems encountered in the distribution of the emergency subsidy or "Ayuda" will not be repeated. The Officer in charge of the City IT Unit submitted the result of the fact-finding committee to the Audit Team. The City returned to the Bureau of Treasury the unexpended balance amounting to P8,970,030.00. The facilitating bank submitted its explanation in writing together with supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to make a comprehensive study and present their recommendations on the possible pre-termination of the outstanding loans for green school and e-vehicle projects of the City using the funds placed in time deposits.
observations.managementAction: The City pre-terminated the outstanding loans for green school and e-vehicle projects using the funds placed in time deposits.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the Chief of Hospital and the Cashier to strictly adhere to Sections 62C and 82 of the Local Treasury Operations Manual of the BLGF by ensuring that all collections of the ONI are deposited intact and promptly and refraining from directly utilizing the same to pay the operating expenses of the hospital. We further recommended that he direct the Chief of Hospital to effectively plan the procurement of highly essential hospital supplies and materials to avoid emergency purchase thereof using the collections of the hospital.
observations.managementAction: Collections from the operations of the ONI are now deposited intact and promptly to the authorized government depository bank. Procurement of highly essential hospital supplies and materials were included in the APP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC-General Services Officer to prepare the Property Inventory Form listing all the insurable properties of the City showing their latest appraised value/valuation, appraisal date, location and other information by extracting the data needed from the latest Report on the Physical Count of PPE, copy furnished the Audit Team not later than April 30 of each year. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to ensure that complete registration documents are submitted by the supplier of motor vehicles before payment is released to the latter.
observations.managementAction: The GSO has requested the 35 units of e-vehicles supplier for the submission of documents needed for their registration. The City Accountant ensured that complete registration documents are submitted by the supplier of motor vehicles before the release of payment to the latter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Budget Officer to study and propose the inclusion in the City’s annual budget the amount of premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties and other assets and ensure its payments to the GSIS.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-GSO prepared the Property Inventory Form listing all the insurable properties of the City showing their latest appraised value/valuation, appraisal date, location and other information. The amount of premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties and other assets were included in the City’s annual budget.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CDRRM Council to:
a. submit written justification to the Audit Team for charging expenses against the prior years’ unexpended balances of LDRRMF and DRRM funds from the National Government; and
b. judiciously plan, manage and monitor the utilization of the previous years’ unexpended balances of LDRRMF and DRRM funds from the National Government by specifying the identified priority disaster management programs and projects in the original and supplemental LDRRMF Investment Plan, if any, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRMO submitted the Acknowledgment Receipts, and Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) per barangay as duly acknowledged by the Barangay Secretary or responsible barangay official and the corresponding Requisition Slips. The Utilization Program for CY 2023 Special Trust Fund out of the unexpended LDRRMF for PPAs under MOOE and CO was submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The LDRRMFIP for CY 2022 did not include the list of PPAs to be charged against the unexpended balances of prior years and the PPAs included were not classified into object or class of expenditures.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. create an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members
to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less, and
who shall be temporarily relieved of all their regular duties to devote their
full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until the same is
completed, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020. The Inventory Committee shall be required to perform the following:
• prepare the PIP to be submitted to the City Mayor for his approval to
ensure that the conduct of inventory is properly and adequately planned;
• submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA Audit Team at least ten 10
calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities;
and
• conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the City in
accordance with the approved PIP and prepare the corresponding
RPCPPE complete with all the required information; and
b. require the General Services Office and the City Accounting Office to update
and reconcile the PCs and PPELCs, respectively, based on the results of the
physical inventory to come up with the correct balances for PPE accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to compute and
journalize the depreciation expenses of the Public Infrastructure Assets totaling
P833,695,971.59, in accordance with IPSAS 7.

We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to prepare
Journal Entry Vouchers to record the reclassification of the various Infrastructure
assets and Buildings amounting to P216,776,852.18 to the appropriate PPE
accounts and provide the corresponding accumulated depreciation expenses.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to coordinate
with the DBP for the submission of updated amortization schedules, whenever
there are drawdowns of loans, to assist her in the monitoring of the long-term
loans payables of the City and to reclassify as current liabilities all amortizations
due and payable within twelve months after the reporting date, in accordance with
IPSAS 1, to ensure fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Accountant to initially record all acquisitions of supplies, materials and
relief goods funded from the LDRRMF in the appropriate inventory accounts;
and
b. General Services Officer to prepare the RSMI duly supported with RIS for
issued supplies and submit them to the Accounting Office for proper recording
to expenses accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor ensure that all procurements in the ensuing
year should be meticulously and judiciously planned and shall be undertaken in
accordance with the approved APP. As such, he shall:

a. direct all the end-user/implementing units of the procuring entity to prepare
their respective PPMP within the prescribed timeline of the preparation of the
annual budget to facilitate their timely review by the City Budget Office and
consolidation into an indicative APP by the BAC Secretariat;
b. require the BAC Secretariat to prepare the indicative APP using the APP form
prescribed by the GPPB, which will be submitted to the BAC for the final
recommendation of the appropriate procurement modality, and finally to the
HoPE for his approval of the APP; and
c. require the BAC to submit the approved APP to the GPPB on or before the end
of January of the budget year.

We also recommend that the City Mayor ensure that the priority development
programs/projects to be funded from the 20% Development Fund in the
succeeding year are adequately planned in terms of their procurement-and-
implementation readiness to ensure that the fund is optimally utilized.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. instruct the City Accountant to compute the liquidated damages that the
supplier must pay due to late deliveries of the 77 e-trikes to the City and
demand that the supplier immediately pay the amount; and
b. file appropriate civil and/or criminal charges and initiate blacklisting
proceedings against the supplier, if warranted, pursuant to the guidelines
issued by the Government Procurement and Policy Board.

We also recommended that the City Inspectorate Committee ensure that the terms of
the contract, particularly as to the place and time of delivery of the procured items as
stated in the Purchase Order, are faithfully adhered to by both parties

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. form a committee consisting of competent City personnel to conduct further
investigation and validation of the reported cases of duplication of payments
to 2,015 beneficiaries and the additional 430 cases of duplications of
beneficiary’s name, barangay address, and bank/e-wallet account number to
ascertain the existence of duplications in the payment of financial assistance
and to recommend measures on how the City could recoup from the
beneficiaries listed in Appendices 10 and 11 the amount mistakenly received
by them or they received in excess of the allowed amount;
b. direct the Officer in charge of the City IT Unit to conduct a thorough
assessment of the City’s existing database of households/families and the
policies and procedures in the preparation of list of beneficiaries to determine
the actual issues that could affect the integrity of the database and generated
reports, and to recommend appropriate measures to ensure that the problems
encountered in the distribution of the emergency subsidy or “Ayuda” will not
be repeated;
c. require the facilitating bank to explain in writing, together with supporting
documents, why there are 197 cases involving financial assistance amounting
to P1,350,000.00 with the same or duplicate transaction reference number as
listed in Appendix 12;
d. direct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to immediately return to the
Bureau of Treasury the unexpended balance of the fund amounting to
P8,970,030.00;
e. require the aforecited personnel or office to prepare and submit the reports on
their findings and/or action taken, copy furnished the COA Audit Team, for
further evaluation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the City
Accountant to make a comprehensive study and present their recommendations
on the possible pre-termination of the outstanding loans for green school and e-
vehicle projects of the City using the funds placed in time deposits.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Chief of Hospital and the
Cashier to strictly adhere to Sections 62C and 82 of the Local Treasury Operations
Manual of the BLGF by ensuring that all collections of the ONI are deposited intact
and promptly and refraining from directly utilizing the same to pay the operating
expenses of the hospital.

We further recommended that he direct the Chief of Hospital to effectively plan the
procurement of highly essential hospital supplies and materials to avoid
emergency purchase thereof using the collections of the hospital.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC- General Services Officer to
demand from the supplier of the 35 units of e-vehicles the submission of
documents needed for their registration or take the appropriate judicial or
extrajudicial remedies against the supplier should it fails to comply with the
demand.

Henceforth, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to
ensure that complete registration documents are submitted by the supplier of
motor vehicles before payment is released to the latter.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Officer-in-Charge of General
Services Office (OIC-GSO) to prepare the Property Inventory Form listing all the
insurable properties of the City showing their latest appraised value/valuation,
appraisal date, location and other information by extracting the data needed from
the latest Report on the Physical Count of PPE, copy furnished the Audit Team
and the GIF, GSIS, not later than April 30 of each year.

We further recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Budget Officer to
study and propose the inclusion in the City’s annual budget the amount of
premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties and other
assets and ensure its payments to the GSIS.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CDRRM Council to:

a. submit written justification to the Audit Team for incurring expenses charged
against the prior years’ unexpended balance of LDRRMF and DRRM funds from
the National Government despite its non-inclusion in the LDRRMF Investment
Plan; and
b. judiciously plan, manage and monitor the utilization of the previous years’
unexpended balances of LDRRMF and DRRM Funds from the National
Government by specifying the identified priority disaster management programs
and projects in the original and supplemental LDRRMF Investment Plan, if any,
in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile their PPE as of December 31, 2021 especially to give priority to PPE accounts which registered significant variances from CY 2018 to CY 2020
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office was continuously working with the Office of the City Accountant for the update and reconciliation of the PPE records.
observations.reason: comprehensive physical count of all the PPE of the City was not yet undertaken in CY 2021.

observations.managementAction: The acquisitions of supplies and materials in GF and SEF totaling P155,249,591.18 in CY 2021 were recorded initially as assets or debited to inventory accounts. Review of the disbursements charged against the LDRRMF showed that of the supplies, materials and relief goods acquired and issued for CY 2021 amounting to P27,799,550.00, only the amount of P17,584,000.00 was supported with RIS.
observations.reason: Not all issuances of supplies were supported with RIS while no SSMI was submitted to the City Accounting Office in CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to draw the necessary Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to correct the deficiencies noted in audit which affected the balances of the Trust Liabilities-DRRM and other accounts.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant submitted the copy of the JEV No. 401-2021-01-009 dated January 07, 2021, and JEV Nos. 100-21-05-3589, 300-21-05-380, 300-21-05-381, 300-21-05-382, and 300-21-05-383, all dated May 06, 2021, for the adjustments made to the Trust Liabilities-DRRM account and other affected accounts.
We also recommended that he instructs the City Treasurer to immediately issue official receipt to recognize the transfer of unexpended DRRM funds from GF to TF and ensure its recording in the Cash Receipts Journal in the proper period to facilitate the timely recognition of the transfer in the City’s books of account.
The unexpended balance of the LDRRMF for CY 2021 amounting to P27,945,913.21 was transferred to the Special Trust Fund at year-end and was properly recorded to the Trust Liabilities-DRRM account in the TF books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the General Services Officer to prepare and issue the necessary Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) to concerned City Officials who are in possession and accountable for the said equipment in accordance with Section 259 of Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer submitted the copy of the signed PARs for the 6 units Airdecon Pure Decontamination and Purification System, 13 units Stihl Mist Blower and 20 units Fuji Mist Blower costing P2,280,000.00, P568,685.00 and P479,814.00, respectively.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant draw the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record under the Loans Receivable - Others account the remaining balances of the financial obligations to the 56 borrowers and to correct the balance of the affected accounts. Henceforth, subsequent grants of loan assistances and receipts of loan assistances under the ICWF Program shall be recorded in the books of accounts using the appropriate accounts for fair presentation of the transactions in the financial statements of the City pursuant to Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant submitted the copy of the JEV No. 300-21-04-324 dated April 20, 2021 for the adjustments made to the Loan Receivables - Others account, and Other Payables account.
Henceforth, all subsequent grants and repayments should be properly recorded in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor limit the advance grant of cash advances to City officials and employees only to circumstances allowed under COA Circular No. 97-002 or when payment thru checks will not be practicable to ensure that government funds are safeguarded from risk of loss or misappropriation and applicable taxes on government money payments to suppliers/creditors are withheld pursuant to Revenue Memorandum Circular (RMC) No. 23-2014.
observations.managementAction: There was no cash advance granted for the purpose of acquiring supplies, materials, relief goods or equipment in CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned focal official of the Local Development Council (LDC) and Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Sangguniang Panlunsod Committee (LDRRMC) to submit to the COA Audit Team for evaluation the copy of the Supplemental Investment Program approved by the Sangguniang Sangguniang Panlunsod covering the expenditures incurred in the implementation of the COVID-19 related Projects and Activities (PPAs) amounting to P16,459,199.83 and P50,843,511.75 charged against the Disaster Mitigation and Preparedness Fund and the Special Trust Funds for the Unexpended LDRRMF in previous years, respectively.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the General
Services Officer to reconcile their respective records, especially to give priority to
PPE accounts which registered significant variances from CYs 2018 to 2020.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. direct the City Accountant to adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in all
procurement of supplies and materials by recording the transactions initially
as an asset using the appropriate inventory accounts prescribed in the
Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units (LGUs); and
b. require the General Services Officer to prepare the Weekly SSMI duly
supported by RIS for submission to the Accounting Office for proper recording
of issuances of inventories.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to draw the
necessary JEVs to correct the deficiencies noted in audit which affected the
balances of the Trust Liabilities – DRRM and other accounts.

We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to
immediately issue official receipt to recognize the transfer of unexpended DRRM
funds from GF to TF and ensure its recording in the Cash Receipts Journal in the
proper period to facilitate timely recognition of the transfer in the City’s books of
account.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw the
necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record as “Disaster Response and
Rescue Equipment” in the GF books the cost and corresponding depreciation of
the six units Airdecon Pure Decontamination and Purification System, 13 units
Stihl Mist Blower and 20 units Fuji Mist Blower.

We also recommended that he instructs the General Services Officer to prepare
and issue the necessary PAR to concerned City Officials who are in possession
and accountable for the said equipment in accordance with Section 259 of GAM
for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Accountant draw the necessary Journal Entry
Voucher (JEV) to record under the Loans Receivable – Others account the
remaining balances of the financial obligations to the City of the 56 borrowers and
to correct the balance of the affected accounts. Henceforth, all subsequent grants
of loan assistances and receipts of loan repayments should be recorded in the
books of accounts using the appropriate accounts for fair presentation of the
transactions in the financial statements of the City pursuant to Revised Chart of
Accounts for LGUs.

We recommended that the City Mayor limit the grant of cash advances to City
officials and employees only to circumstances allowed under COA Circular
No. 97-002 or when payment thru checks will not be practicable to ensure that
government funds are safeguarded from risk of loss or misappropriation and
applicable taxes due from government money payments to suppliers/creditors are
withheld pursuant to RMC No. 23-2014.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned focal official of the
LDC and Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Committee (LDRRMC) to
submit to the COA Audit Team for evaluation the copy of the Supplemental
Investment Program approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod Resolution
covering the expenditures incurred in the implementation of the COVID-19 related
PPAs amounting to P16,459,199.83 and P50,843,511.75 charged against the
Disaster Mitigation and Preparedness Fund and Special Trust Funds for the
Unexpended LDRRMF in Previous Years, respectively.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to facilitate the
immediate submission to the COA Audit Team of the lacking documentary
requirements on COVID-19 expenses totaling P52,731,151.40 such as the
approved Purchase Requests, Purchase Orders, Sales Invoices, Acceptance and
Inspection Reports, and Pictures taken during the receipt and inspection of the
goods, to prevent the issuance of a Notice of Suspension for the said
transactions.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) to submit within 10 days from receipt hereof to the COA Audit Team the
Mayor’s/Business Permit, OSS, and Income/Business Tax Return of Grab-It-All
General Merchandise, Eight Star General Merchandise, and Redvaron Trading
necessary to establish the validity of the expenses incurred by the City amounting to
P20,675,196.00, otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be issued for the said
transactions.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all the property and equipment of the City at least once a year and prepare and submit the corresponding RPCPPE to the Auditor’s Office on or before January 31 of the immediate succeeding year.
observations.managementAction: The RPCPPE of the City for CY 2020 was submitted to the COA Auditor’s Office on March 23, 2021.
observations.reason: Several limitations such as lack of manpower for supplies and property records management, volume of transactions of the City, and unavailability of records for various PPE accounts acquired in previous years hindered the conduct of complete reconciliation of the Accounting Office’ and General Services Office' records for PPE accounts. We also recommended that the City Mayor instruct the General Services Officer and the City Accountant to continue their efforts of reconciling and updating their respective records of the PPE accounts based on the RPCPPE and considering the materiality of these accounts relative to the total assets, these concerned City officials be directed to formulate a system to effectively reduce the variance in their records which may include prioritizing those unreconciled PPE accounts with significant balances and those recently acquired as these have the highest probability of having available supporting documents to facilitate the inventory- taking. Several deficiencies in the RPCPPE for CY 2018 such as misclassification of PPE items and respective inclusion of lump-sum beginning balances had been corrected in the RPCPPE for CY 2020. However, the Accounting Office and General Services Office were still unable to completely reconcile their respective records and for CY 2020, the discrepancy had increased to P338,659,065.24.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to reclassify all time deposits with maturity of three months or less from the date of acquisition from Investments to Cash and Cash Equivalents accounts, in accordance with IPSAS 2 for a more appropriate reporting and classification of the City’s short term investments in the Statement of Financial Position, Statement of Cash Flows, and Notes to Financial Statement. Moreover, ensure that the reported amount of the time deposit accounts reflect the updated balance in order to fairly present the year-end balance of the affected accounts.

Moreover, ensure that the reported amount of the time deposit accounts reflect the updated balance in order to fairly present the year-end balance of the affected accounts. The interest income due for transfer to the General Fund as of December 31, 2020 in the amount of P22,204.09 were properly recorded as a credit to the “Due to Other Funds” account in the Trust Fund books and as a debit and credit to the “Due from Other Funds” account and “Interest Income” account, respectively, in the General Fund books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to debit Due from Other Funds account and credit Other Payables account and debit and credit the Other Payables and Due to Other Funds accounts, respectively, in the TF books, all in the amount of P814,136.26 to correctly recognize the interest earned in Calendar Year (CY) 2019. Moreover, a GAD Database is now being maintained under the Office of the City Planning and Development Officer consisting of Community Based Monitoring System (CBMS) and barangay sex-disaggregated data.

We also recommended that the City Mayor instruct the General Services Officer and the City Accountant to continue their efforts of reconciling and updating their respective records of the PPE accounts based on the RPCPPE, and considering the materiality of these accounts relative to the total assets. Moreover, direct these concerned City officials to formulate a system to effectively reduce the variance in their records which may include prioritizing those unreconciled PPE accounts with significant balances and those recently acquired as these have the highest probability of having available supporting documents to facilitate the inventory- taking.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to reclassify all time deposits with maturity of three months or less from the date of acquisition from Investments to Cash and Cash Equivalents accounts, in accordance with IPSAS 2 for a more appropriate reporting and classification of the City’s short term investments in the Statement of Financial Position, Statement of Cash Flows, and Notes to Financial Statements. Moreover, ensure that the reported amount of the time deposit accounts reflect the updated balance in order to fairly present the year-end balance of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Office of the City Engineer and the Office of the City Accountant continue performing the necessary procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 to facilitate the completion of the reconciliation of their respective records for local road and road network system and the preparation of the working paper which will serve as the basis of the latter in the recording of the balance of P124,496,425.18 in the books of accounts. Subsequent adjustments should also be made for the depreciation to reflect the existing assets in its used condition based on the estimated useful life prescribed by the COA or on Management’s assessment of the asset's service potential, taking into consideration its design, kind of users and volume of users.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to debit Due from Other Funds account and credit Prior Year Adjustments account in the GF books and debit and credit the Other Payables and Due to Other Funds accounts, respectively, in the TF books, all in the amount of P814,136.26 to correctly recognize the interest earned in Calendar Year (CY) 2019. Sequentially, the City Treasurer shall immediately transfer said amount from Trust to General fund bank accounts and reciprocally effect the correct entry for the transfer of said amount from one fund to another.

Henceforth, we recommended that all interests earned from TF’s bank deposits be credited to Due to Other Funds account in the TF books and the Due from Other Funds reciprocal account be debited in the GF books, with the corresponding credit to Miscellaneous Income at the time the interests are earned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant, the City Treasurer and the Human Resource Management Officer to coordinate with each other and reconcile the unsettled/unremitted/unaccounted balances of Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth, as well as the variances in the amount remitted for the applicable months from January to November 2018. Based on the results of validation/reconciliation of balances, the Accounting Office shall prepare the necessary correcting/adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the said liability accounts in their records. Also, effect/make immediate action to remit the amount determined to be due to the concerned agencies.
observations.managementAction:
Remittances to Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), Home Development Mutual Fund (HDMF), and PhilHealth in January to November 2019 from regular payroll payments were remitted in full and promptly in accordance with RA No. 8291, RA No. 9679, RA No. 10606, respectively.
The year-end balance included amounts withheld in December 2019 and timing differences.
observations.reason:
The year-end balance of the Due to BIR account of P27,928,666.01 was comprised mostly of the amount withheld in December 2019 of P27,874,613.46 or 99.81 per cent thereof, while the remaining balance of P54,052.55 or 0.19 per cent pertained to the unremitted accounts withheld in January to November 2019 and in the prior years.

The amount of P7,902,688.29 or 42.94 per cent of the aggregate year-end balances of the Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG, and Due to PhilHealth accounts with no further action was due to timing differences in the billing and remittances and require updating of personnel information by the concerned national government agencies for their electronic billing system.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Office verify the correctness and propriety of the reported PPE items in the submitted Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for CY 2018 and ensure that all items enlisted therein are actually existing and are in unserviceable condition. Thereafter, submit the IIRUP to the Office of the City Accountant for the determination of the corresponding accumulated depreciation of the Property (IIRUP) for CY 2018 and ensure that all items enlisted therein are actually existing and are in unserviceable condition. Thereafter, submit the IIRUP to the Office of the City Accountant for the determination of the corresponding accumulated depreciation/amortization for the applicable items and facilitate their disposal.
observations.managementAction: Disposal of assets was undertaken in the first semester of CY 2020 which included the unserviceable PPE reported in the IIRUP for CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accounting and Internal Audit Services Office locate the documents pertaining to the reported liability totaling P722,999.68 and trace the origin date of the transaction to establish the correctness of the amount and the propriety of the account title used. Based on the results of such validation, prepare the necessary adjusting entry/entries to ensure correctness of the relevant financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Necessary adjustment was made in the books of accounts in CY 2019 to correct the balances of the affected accounts under Journal Entry Voucher No. 300-19-05-468.

JEV Nos. 100-19-03 and 300-19-03 were drawn on March 06, 2019 to record the adjustments for the stale checks amounting to P20,000.00 and P80,951.09 in the General Fund and Trust Fund books, respectively.
The Office of the City Treasurer had imposed stricter monitoring of issued checks. Payees were also notified about the status of the checks due to them to minimize the occurrences of stale checks.
The documents to support the reconciling item of P120,000.00 was furnished to the Office of the City Accountant which served the basis for the recording in the books of accounts the necessary adjustment under JEV No. 300-2019-02-188 dated February 28, 2019.
Documents to support the other reconciling items amounting to P86,605.50 had been secured and served the basis of the following JEVs: JEV No. 100-19-01-406 dated January 31, 2019 for the unrecorded cost of check booklet of P2,500.00; JEV No. 100-19-03 dated March 06, 2019 for the overstatement in the recorded deposit of P9,584.00; JEV No. 100-19-03 dated March 06, 2019 for the unrecorded withdrawal amounting to P6,139.50; JEV No. 200-19-03 dated March 06, 2019 for the unrecorded withdrawal amounting to P7,371.00; and JEV No. 300-2019-01-012 dated January 11, 2019 for the unrecorded receipts of hospital claims amounting to P112,200.00.
Necessary adjustments were already taken-up in the books of accounts for the valid reconciling items under the above-mentioned JEVs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the Secretariat, Bids and Awards Committee, City Engineer and General Services Officer to observe strict compliance with the relevant provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 on the timely submission of copies of contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents to facilitate prompt review and evaluation of infrastructure projects and procurement of goods of the City, and deficiencies found in audit could be immediately communicated to the concerned officials.
We also recommended that the City Mayor require the CGSO and other City officials/employees in charge of accepting deliveries of procured items to comply strictly with the regular and timely submission of Notice of Deliveries to the Office of the Auditor within 24 hours upon acceptance of any procured item/s, pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-010, to enable the Auditor to inspect and attest that deliveries were in accordance with the specifications and made within the terms and conditions set forth in the purchase orders.
observations.managementAction:
Submissions to the COA Auditor's Office of advance copy of contracts/purchase orders and goods procured were regularly performed by the City Bids and other City officials. The APP-CSE for CY 2020 was submitted to the Procurement Service DBM on December 26, 2019.
The APP of the City for CY 2020 was prepared by the BAC Secretariat using the form prescribed by the GPPB.
Inspection Reports for goods procured and delivered in 2019 were regularly performed by the GSO.
observations.reason: The documents to support the contracts/purchase orders and goods procured were made available to the Audit Team only when the disbursement vouchers were transmitted from the Office of the City Accountant or upon post-audit of the transactions. There were still several occasions wherein the Audit Team was notified late of the delivery of goods in 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to formulate and adopt more aggressive collection strategies and consider availing of the remedies provided under the collection and reduction of the delinquent real property taxes. Based on the results, ensure appropriate follow-up to collect and reduce delinquent real property tax accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Certified List of All Real Property Tax Delinquencies (CLARPTD) for CY 2019 showed total uncollected real property taxes of P186,858,693.44 which was lower by 2.09 per cent as compared to the total tax delinquency in CY 2018 of P190,861,669.04.
observations.reason: Total appropriations for the relevant programs implemented and collection activities resulted in a decrease in total delinquencies by 2.09% compared to CY 2018; however the city still needs to adopt more aggressive measures and there remain opportunities to further reduce delinquencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor ensure that the 20 per cent Development Fund is optimally utilized by seeing to it that adequate planning during the identification of priority development Project Programs and Activities (PPAs) and formulation of Annual Investment Plan (AIP), and close monitoring of project implementation thereafter, were undertaken by the concerned personnel of the City Government.
observations.managementAction: Total appropriations for the 20 per cent Development Fund in the CY 2019 amounted to P203,600,000.00, while obligations charged against the Fund amounted to P200,346,950.00. The total project completion for the year amounted to P172,029,668.28 or 84.49 per cent of the total appropriations for the Fund.
observations.reason: Total project completion for the year amounted to P172,029,668.28 or 84.49 per cent of the total appropriations for the Fund. There was one project that was not implemented during the year in the amount of P1,000,000.00, and four projects that were partially completed in the aggregate cost of R30,570,331.72.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Office (GSO) follow-up with the Land Transportation Office (LTO) the government car plates for the 12 motor vehicles acquired from CYs 2013 to 2015 and install the markings prescribed under Item V(2) of COA Circular No. 75-6 dated November 7, 1975 on the 12 motor vehicles to reduce/eliminate the possible risk of misuse thereof.
observations.managementAction: Regular follow-up of the government plates with the LTO was undertaken by the GSO, and temporary markings were installed/placed on the 12 government motor vehicles.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
(a) look into the matter and if warranted, impose appropriate administrative sanctions to those personnel for committing the acts of: (1) reporting the undelivered medicines as accepted and inspected; (2) not reflecting in the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) the true and actual dates of deliveries as per Acknowledgement Receipts (ARs); and (3) not incorporating the undertaking of the supplier and the ARs in the DV, which caused the City Officials to certify and approve the same; and
(b) Direct the Accountant to collect from the supplier the corresponding liquidated damages of medicines costing P259,792.00 for late delivery and cost of the undelivered medicines of P388,500.00 within 15 calendar days from receipt hereof. Absence of documented action shall mean the issuance of the corresponding Notice of Disallowance; and
(c) Direct the City Accountant to properly scrutinize subsequent transactions to prevent payment of fraudulent claims.
observations.managementAction:
Complete delivery of the medicines costing P388,500.00 was made on July 15, 2019, while liquidated damages on account of the late delivery in the amount of P90,600.67 was paid by the Supplier on July 31, 2019 under Official Receipt No. 1235669.
An investigation on the case had been conducted by Management. Moreover, the City Legal Officer had sent a communication letter to the Supplier to inform the latter of the issue and to request also for a written justification.
No occurrence of the similar case had been observed or come into the attention of the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require all the concerned department/unit heads, as the end-users of the procuring entity, to observe strict compliance on the preparation and submission of their respective PPMPs within the deadlines set forth for the preparation of the City's proposed budget for the ensuing year, and to facilitate also the timely preparation of its APP.
We also recommended that the BAC Secretariat, based on the submitted and reviewed PPMPs, prepare the APP in the form prescribed by the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB), indicating therein among others, the mode of procurement to be adopted and the schedule for each procurement activities and contract implementation to ensure that its procurement activities are properly/adequately planned.
observations.managementAction:
The Annual Procurement Plan - Common Supplies and Equipment (APP-CSE) of the City for CY 2020 was submitted to the Procurement Service of Department of Budget and Management (PS DBM) on December 26, 2019.
The APP of the City for CY 2020 was prepared by the BAC Secretariat using the form prescribed by the GPPB.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Planning and Development Coordinator and GAD Focal Point System to:
(a) Establish and maintain a GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation. The GAD database, which will form part of the overall management information system of the City; and
(b) Undertake gender analysis to identify existing issues in the locality and ensure that the programs, projects and activities to be included in the GAD Plan and Budget are responsive to the prevailing issues.
observations.managementAction:
Management had included a training program on the generation and management of sex-disaggregated data in the City’s GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2019, but the same did not push through because of the unavailability of the invited speaker/trainer.
Management has sex-disaggregated data based on the Community-Based Monitoring System (CBMS) and Ecological Profile of the various offices of the City, but these were not yet organized into a GAD database.
observations.reason: Management had not yet established a GAD Database. The GAD focal person acknowledged the deficiencies noted and assured compliance with the recommendations. The training included in the GPB for CY 2019 was not implemented due to unavailability of the invited trainer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LDRRM Council to identify and incorporate, under a separate caption, in the LDRRMF Investment Plan the projects and activities to be charged out of the unexpended LDRRM Fund of previous years, in compliance with the said COA Circular and in order to effectively monitor and evaluate the management and utilization of the Fund.
observations.managementAction: Utilization of the LDRRMF Special Trust Fund in CY 2019 in the amount of P30,672,161.92 were supported by programs and activities in the LDRRMF Investment Plan for the year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to coordinate with each other and reconcile their respective records to verify and account for the discrepancy found in audit of P3,334,900.86. Thereafter, prepare the necessary correcting/adjusting entries and effect immediate remittance of the amount determined to be due to the BIR. The corresponding penalties and/or interests, if there are any, should be on the personal account of the responsible personnel.
We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to observe strict compliance in the remittance of taxes withheld from compensation and payments to suppliers/creditors within 10 days after the end of each month, pursuant to BIR Revenue Regulations No. 2-98.
observations.managementAction:
The year-end balance of the Due to BIR account of P27,928,666.01 was comprised mostly of the amount withheld in December 2019 of P27,874,613.46 or 99.81 per cent thereof, while the remaining balance of P54,052.55 or 0.19 per cent pertained to the unremitted accounts withheld in January to November 2019 and in the prior years.
Taxes withheld from January to November 2019 were remitted promptly by the concerned office to the BIR.

the individual balances of the prior years’ Accounts Payable to establish their
validity and adjust accordingly to reflect the true financial position of the City as

Government and the 97 barangays of the City are promptly remitted.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to adopt more
effective strategies in collecting tax delinquencies and strictly enforce the
remedies provided under Sections 176 and 256 of RA No. 7160.
